text:
What Accenture's 11,000 Layoffs Reveal About the Future of Your Job
In recent interviews, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei has been predicting a 10-50 percent unemployment rate among entry-level white collar workers as a result of corporate America's embrace of artificial intelligence. 
Such predictions obviously send chills down the spines of knowledge workers and IT professionals everywhere. But what is actually happening now? 
Last September, Dublin-based global consulting titan Accenture fired 11,000 employees that it said were untrainable in artificial intelligence. This was part of a $865 million restructuring involving the adoption of AI. For the record, Accenture has more than 700,000 employees throughout the world.

Ever tried to cook a 7-course Thanksgiving dinner for 50 people, one plate at a time, as they walk through the door?
That’s Real-Time Processing. It’s fast, it’s exciting, but man... it’s stressful, expensive, and one tiny mistake creates a massive bottleneck.
Now, imagine the Midnight Chef.
While the rest of the world sleeps, the Midnight Chef is in a quiet kitchen. They aren't rushing. They are prepping ingredients in massive crates, slow-cooking the broth for hours, and organizing everything into perfect containers.
By the time the sun rises, the work is done. It’s high-volume, perfectly executed, and cost-effective.
That is the power of Batch Deployment. 
In the world of "Real-Time everything," we often forget that Batch Processing is the unsung hero. It’s the engine behind your monthly bank statements, the training of massive AI models, and the heavy-duty data cleaning that keeps businesses sane.
I’ve put together a deep dive into why

I used to think that teaching an AI a new skill was like performing open-heart surgery.
To make it better at medical coding or creative writing, you had to crack open the entire model, move billions of "neurons" around, and pray you didn't break its common sense in the process. It was expensive, slow, and frankly, a bit of a nightmare.
Then I discovered Adaptors.
Imagine you have a world-class athlete. Instead of retraining their entire body every time they switch sports, you just give them the right gear.
 Running? Put on the sprinting shoes.
 ■ Swimming? Grab the goggles.
 Cycling? Strap on the helmet.
The athlete’s core strength stays the same—you’re just adding a "lightweight backpack" of specialized tools.
In the world of Large Language Models (LLMs), this is what we call PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning).
I’ve been diving deep into this lately, from the classic Houlsby methods to the mathematical wizardry of LoRA and the high-precision "magnitude-direction" split of DoRA.
The result? I can now train a massive 70B model on a single home GPU. No massive server farm required. Just a tiny "plug-in" module that represents less than 1% of the model’s weight, but carries 100% of the new skill.
The era of "one-size-fits-all" AI is over. We’re moving toward a modular future where your AI can swap skills as fast as you can swap a USB drive.

Anatomy of an Edge AI Agent: Intelligence Where It Matters
Imagine a small device on a factory floor.
No cloud. No powerful servers.
Just a local system, quietly listening to machine vibrations.
Something changes. A pattern shifts.
There is no time to send data to the cloud and wait.
A decision must happen now.
This is where an Edge AI agent comes to life.
At its core, every Edge AI agent is built on four key components:
• The Model → the brain, small but powerful 
• The Inference Engine → runs the model efficiently on real hardware 
• The Data Interface → connects the system to the real world 
• The Action Logic → decides what to do 
Together, they form a simple but powerful loop:
 sense → understand → act
At the edge, this loop must be fast, efficient, and autonomous.
Why This Matters
When everything runs locally:
• No cloud latency 
• No dependency on connectivity 
• No delays between perception and action 
Just real-time intelligence.
This is what transforms a system from a passive observer…
 into an active agent.
"From Prediction to Action"
A model alone is not enough.
What really matters is what happens after the prediction.
• A machine detects abnormal vibrations → does it stop? 
• A car sees a pedestrian → does it brake? 
• A wearable detects risk → does it alert? 
This is where intelligence becomes behavior

AI is a hot topic of discussion across all areas of life, raising questions about the future of jobs and society. Recently, I delved into "Nexus," a book by Yuval Noah Harari, known for his works "Sapiens" and "Homo Deus." Although I purchased it over a year ago, I only recently had the opportunity to read it, and it has provided valuable insights.
This article summarizes key points from the book and outlines actions we must take to ensure that algorithms and AI do not pursue harmful goals. A crucial question arises: how do we establish the right objectives for AI? Harari presents some ideas, but I believe it will take the collaboration of the brightest minds to explore this further.
I recall a TED talk by the founder of "Replika," the AI companion app, who suggested that may be this whole time, we have been directing AI toward the wrong goal—"Engagement," driven by human greed. Imagine if the focus shifted to maximizing overall human happiness instead

Its hard to stay motivated all the time.
Self doubt, overthinking, feeling lost… it’s normal.
Not every day is productive.
Not every day you feel like doing anything.
But small things change everything.

Showing up even on bad days.
Trying again after failure.
Not quitting when it gets hard.

Thats what actually makes the difference.
You dont need to be perfect every day.
You just need to not give up.

Every time I poured my heart into 5-6 rounds of interviews and faced rejection, it felt like a punch in the gut. The sleepless nights, the endless preparation, all for nothing.

But looking back, I realize it wasnt nothing. It was the Universes way of saying, “Not this one, something better is on the way.”

Every single time, Ive been shown why that rejection happened.

Doors I thought I wanted to walk through were shut, only to have the right ones swing open.

The kind that aligned with my growth, my values, and my happiness.

At first, it stung. It hurt deeply. But now, when things dont go as planned, I dont panic.

I dont question my worth. I sit back, breathe, and trust. The Universe knows.

I know there's another plan waiting.Something bigger, better, and just for me.

To anyone feeling the weight of rejection: trust that the closed doors are protecting you from something you cant see right now.

Your path is being cleared for something even more beautiful.

To everyone who's still looking for a job...
I see you. I feel you. 
Every rejection email feels like a punch in the gut, and every 'We'll get back to you' sounds more like 'You'll never hear from us.'

But I want you to know, you're not alone in this. 
According to a study, 80% of jobseekers struggle with anxiety and self-doubt during their search. It's normal to feel lost, but it's not the end.

Take breaks, breathe, and remember, this doesn't define you. Your worth is not tied to an offer letter. 
Your mental health matters more than any job.

Sometimes, we forget that a companys brand name doesnt define someones talent. Its easy to get caught up in the 'big company = big talent' mindset, but that's not always the case.

Ive had the privilege of working with people from smaller companies (lesser known) who blow me away with their skills and dedication. They dont need a fancy title or a famous brand behind them to prove their worth.

I've seen the other side too—people in top-tier companies feeling lost, overwhelmed, or stuck, even though the world sees them as 'successful'.

Lets stop attaching someones value to the company they work for. Freshers especially need to hear this—skills are what matter, not the size of the company behind them.

At the end of the day, happiness and growth dont come from a brand name, they come from doing what you love and constantly improving your craft.

So when I left a toxic work environment, I told my manager a simple thing and felt so good 
I just said-
'Hope your son gets a manager like you.
I hope that the manager behaves the same way as you did with me.
Thank you.'

Now tell me 1 thing-

She always said that she was a great manager.
Why will she get offended?

I just told her that I wish her son would get a manager like she was.

If you felt bad, then that means you were a bad manager and now you know it. 
If you feel good, then take it as a blessing for your son and you'll really want someone to treat your son/daughter in the same way.

She cannot be even angry with me else it'll prove that she was not a 'great' manager.

Muskan - 1
Manager - 0

Muskan -> Aura +100000000

(Fictional message unfortunately :(
)

Hope you all become the people that your sons/daughters will like to work under 
There are a lot of bad people/things, bring a small change and break the chain :)
